naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the Pierpont branch through mother Mary Elizabeth Blake's ancestry (H00388 → HD1556 → HD1565).

notable.notable_blurb: Set the founding of Alcoholics Anonymous in motion

narrative_blocks.header: "Rowland H." in the AA Big Book
narrative_blocks.detail: Jung's verdict led Rowland to the Oxford Group, a Christian organization. In 1934 he arranged the release of Ebby Thacher from commitment in Vermont. Ebby then carried the Oxford Group message to Bill Wilson in Brooklyn, directly setting in motion the founding of AA. Rowland appears as "Rowland H." in the AA Big Book (p.26). He never joined AA himself. His papers are at the Rhode Island Historical Society (MSS 483) and the Beinecke Library at Yale. He served in the RI State Senate 1914-1916, and as a WWI captain in the US Army Chemical Warfare Service. Taft School; Yale 1903 (Elihu Club). (AA Big Book; AA history; Yale Beinecke; Rhode Island Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Rowland Hazard III ("Roy") struggled with alcoholism for years. In 1926 he consulted Carl Jung in Zurich, who told him only a vital spiritual experience could help — a diagnosis that set off a chain of events leading directly to the founding of Alcoholics Anonymous.

narrative_blocks.header: Carl Jung told him medicine couldn’t help
narrative_blocks.body: Jung’s 1926 verdict — that only a vital spiritual experience could save him — sent Hazard to the Oxford Group. When he later arranged Ebby Thacher’s release from a Vermont commitment, Thacher carried that message to Bill Wilson, and Alcoholics Anonymous followed; Hazard himself never joined.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the family name of his mother Mary Pierrepont Bushnell Hazard (HD1577).

narrative_blocks.detail: St. George's School Newport RI; Yale B.A. 1915 (Wolf's Head). WWI service included Mexican border intervention. Solvay Process Company 1915-1923 (rising to assistant to the president). RI State Senator 1930-1932; RI State Treasurer 1938-1940. WWII: Chief, New England War Production Board priorities section 1941-1943; Civil Affairs Officer in Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples; Liaison Officer to Chief Quartermaster, US Army — played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945. Published "Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction" (1949). His papers (20 linear feet, 1893-1979) are at the Rhode Island Historical Society (RIHS MSS 483 sg 9). (Rhode Island Historical Society; Yale; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: Served as Rhode Island State Treasurer 1938-1940; ran as Republican nominee for US Senate 1948 (lost to Theodore F. Green). The 1948 race was the high-water mark of his political career after South Kingstown Town Council, RI State Senate, and an unsuccessful 1932 US House run.

As Liaison Officer to the Chief Quartermaster of the U.S. Army based in England from February 1944, he played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945 after Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples Civil Affairs assignments.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote a 1949 book on civil affairs reconstruction
narrative_blocks.body: Published 'Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction' (1949) based on his WWII Liaison Officer experience. Also co-authored a 1951 American Fisheries Society paper on modification of the sexual cycle in brook trout — his family's American Fish Culture Company connection.

He enlisted Troop D 1st NY Cavalry as a private in October 1915 at twenty-three; called to active duty for the Mexican border intervention June 1916; commissioned 2nd Lt. then promoted 1st Lt. in the 14th U.S. Cavalry at Del Rio TX. Discharged February 1919 — never went overseas in WWI.

education.notes: — Wolf's Head Senior Society. Psi Upsilon Fraternity.

orbit_connections.name: Gov. Henry Baldwin Harrison (via Frances Louisa Blake Osborn)
orbit_connections.relationship: Arthur Dimon Osborn — son of US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne, and husband of HD1572 Frances Louisa Blake — had a sister Elizabeth who married Governor Henry Baldwin Harrison of Connecticut
orbit_connections.notes: Frances Louisa Blake (HD1572) married Arthur Dimon Osborn. Arthur's father was US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne (CT). Arthur's sister Elizabeth married Henry Baldwin Harrison, Governor of Connecticut 1885-1887. Their son Thomas Burr Osborn (chemist) and Arthur Sherwood Osborn were grandchildren of Eli Whitney Blake. (Wikipedia on Eli Whitney Blake)

notable.notable_blurb: Blake stone crusher inventor, nephew of Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Patented the Blake stone crusher for road-building
narrative_blocks.detail: Blake graduated Yale in 1816 and trained at Litchfield Law School before abandoning law to work at his uncle Eli Whitney's armory in Whitneyville. He also held the first US mortise lock patent (1833, Blake Brothers) and served as president of the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences. His son Henry Taylor Blake (HD1568) went on to build New Haven's park system — the stone-crusher's roads and the park commissioner's parks both serving the same expanding American city. (Wikipedia; research_notes)
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Whitney Blake patented his stone crusher in 1857. The first commercially successful machine of its kind, it made macadam roads practical on a national scale and is credited as foundational to American infrastructure. He is in the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

narrative_blocks.header: He left law for his uncle’s armory
narrative_blocks.body: Litchfield-trained, Blake abandoned the law for Eli Whitney’s armory; his 1833 mortise lock was the first such American patent, and his 1857 stone crusher put him in the National Inventors Hall of Fame. He died at ninety-one.

documents.title: 1830 letter from George Washington Blake (Boston) to Elihu Blake Jr. (NYC) mentioning Eli Whitney Blake
documents.description: Stampless letter from G.W. Blake (1803-?, brother) in Boston to Elihu Blake Jr. (1793-1875, brother) at No. 5 Park Place, NYC. Mentions Eli Whitney Blake by name in connection with Mr. W. prescribing 'be married before the first day of next August' for his malady. Also mentions parents Pa (Elihu Blake Sr.) enjoys tolerable health and mother (Elizabeth Fay Whitney) is well; siblings Maria, Elizabeth, Frances, Josiah Whitney, John Adams; uncle Blake; Aunt Whitney in New Haven; the September 1830 boiler explosion on the steamboat United States. Authors not built in JSON per Jalapeño Rule but documented here for family-correspondence record.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law there before abandoning law for the Whitney Armory.

career.organization: Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences

cross_connections.display_label: was her mother-in-law; Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two children to disease in early 1836
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1836 Eliza Maria buried two of her young children within eight weeks — six-year-old Robert Pierrepont (HD1569) on January 17 and two-year-old Eliza Maria (HD1571) on March 13. She gave the name Eliza Maria again to a daughter born five years later (HD1576), passing down the name from the daughter she had lost.

burial.plot_notes: Pine Grove Cemetery, Westborough MA (FA cemetery #91450) — not yet built as CEM entry.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary War surgeon; patriarch of the Blake-Whitney family
notable.primary_url_label: FindAGrave: Pine Grove Cemetery (his burial site)

military_service.notes: Per family genealogy. Born 1764 — would have been a teenager during the war (about 12-19 years old 1776-1783); surgeon assistant role plausible. Service details require verification.

narrative_blocks.header: He fathered a family of inventors
narrative_blocks.body: Elihu Blake married Elizabeth Fay Whitney, sister of the cotton-gin inventor, and their household became a seedbed of invention. Their sons devised a celebrated stone crusher, dental appliances, and a corkscrew design.

narrative_blocks.header: Tradition makes him a Revolutionary War surgeon
narrative_blocks.body: Family genealogy holds that Elihu Blake served as a surgeon in the Revolution, though he was only a teenager during the war and the role may have been as a surgeon's apprentice. He lived to eighty-five at Westborough, Massachusetts.

notable.notable_blurb: Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother invented the cotton gin
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth was the sister of Eli Whitney, whose cotton gin transformed American agriculture. After his death her sons took over the running of the Whitney Armory at New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: She passed an inventive streak to her sons
narrative_blocks.body: She married Dr. Elihu Blake and bore ten children, several of whom inherited the family talent for invention. Four of her sons came to New Haven to work in the Whitney Armory and the later Blake Brothers firm.
